LO 39G · UNE COM Anatomy · Dr. Willard

The Scalp

Describe & demonstrate the layers, composition, blood supply, venous drainage, lymphatics, and innervation of the scalp.

Layers Histology Arteries Veins Lymphatics Nerves Quiz
Study view
Cleaner layout, same anatomy content
Background
01 / Composition

§ Layers of the Scalp

The scalp is composed of five layers, the first three of which are connected intimately and move as a unit (e.g., when wrinkling the forehead). The word SCALP itself serves as a mnemonic for its layers.

SSkinEpidermis & dermis; hair, sweat & sebaceous glands
CConnective tissueDense, vascularized subcutaneous layer
AAponeurosisEpicranial (galea) aponeurotica
LLoose CTSubgaleal space; "danger area"
PPericraniumPeriosteum of the cranial bones
S

Skin

Thin (except occipital region). Contains many sweat glands, sebaceous glands, and hair follicles. Has abundant arterial supply and good venous/lymphatic drainage. Epidermis is keratinized stratified squamous epithelium.

C

Connective Tissue (Dense Subcutaneous)

The thick, dense, richly vascularized subcutaneous layer (hypodermis + superficial fascia). Well supplied with cutaneous nerves. Arteries course in this layer. The dense CT firmly binds skin to the aponeurosis — arterial walls are anchored here, limiting their ability to constrict when cut, causing profuse bleeding.

A

Aponeurosis (Epicranial / Galea Aponeurotica)

Broad, strong, tendinous sheet covering the calvaria. The flat intermediate tendon of the occipitofrontalis muscle (frontal belly anteriorly → aponeurosis → occipital belly posteriorly). Has longitudinal collagenous fibers running between the frontalis and occipitalis. Laterally attaches to temporoparietalis and auricular muscles. All parts innervated by CN VII.

L

Loose Areolar Tissue (Subgaleal Space)

Sponge-like layer with potential spaces that can distend with fluid from injury or infection. Allows free movement of the scalp proper (layers 1–3) over the calvaria. Contains emissary veins connecting scalp veins to dural sinuses — this is the "danger area" because infections can spread to the brain via these connections.

P

Pericranium (Periosteum)

Dense connective tissue forming the external periosteum of the neurocranium. Firmly attached to bone but can be stripped in living persons, except at cranial sutures where it is continuous with suture fibrous tissue. Has poor osteogenic properties in adults.

⚠
"Danger Area" — Subgaleal Space: Infections in the subgaleal space can spread across the scalp because the loose CT allows free movement. Emissary veins pass through the cranial bone from scalp veins to dural sinuses (e.g., cavernous sinus, superior sagittal sinus). An infection here can therefore reach the brain. Surgeons suture the galea to the pericranium to prevent infection from spreading in this space.
Anson AtlasScalp Layers — Dissection
Dissection showing layers A–G: A = Epidermis & dermis, B = Hypodermis, C = Subcutaneous fascia, D = Galea aponeurotica, E = Subgaleal space, F = Periosteum, G = Cranial bone.
Gray's AtlasScalp & Cranial Meninges
S = Skin, C = Connective tissue (dense), A = Aponeurotic layer, L = Loose connective tissue, P = Pericranium. Note emissary veins, diploic veins, and superior sagittal sinus.
02 / Histology

§ Histological Section & Schematic

HistologyVertical Section of Scalp
Histological section: epidermis (stratified squamous keratinized) → dermis (with sweat glands & hair follicles) → hypodermis → superficial fascia (irregular dense CT with scattered globs) → galea aponeurotica (longitudinally aligned fibers) → subgaleal space → cranial periosteum.
SchematicSchematic Cross-Section
Schematic showing: Cutis (epidermis + dermis), Subcutis (hypodermis), Fascia superficialis, Galea aponeurotica, Cavum subgaleale, Pericranium, Os parietale, Dura mater, Sinus sagittalis superior, Falx cerebri.
🔬
Key Histological Distinction: The superficial fascia (layer C) has irregular, dense connective tissue with collagen fibers running in multiple directions. The galea aponeurotica (layer A) has longitudinal collagenous fibers that line up in rows, running between the frontalis and occipitalis muscles. These layers look very different under the microscope despite appearing similar grossly.
03 / Arterial Supply

§ Arteries of the Scalp

The scalp has an extremely rich blood supply. Arteries course within layer 2 (the dense subcutaneous CT between skin and galea). They anastomose freely with adjacent arteries and across the midline. Because arterial walls are firmly anchored in the dense CT, they cannot constrict when cut, causing profuse bleeding from scalp wounds.

Arterial Supply of the Scalp

ArterySourceTerritory
Superficial temporal a.Terminal branch of external carotid ECALateral scalp; frontal & parietal branches supply most of the temporal and parietal regions
Occipital a.External carotid ECAPosterior scalp (occipital region)
Posterior auricular a.External carotid ECAScalp posterior to the auricle
Supratrochlear a.Ophthalmic a. ← internal carotid ICAMedial forehead/anterior scalp
Supra-orbital a.Ophthalmic a. ← internal carotid ICAForehead and anterior scalp
ArteriesArteries of the Scalp
Arteries of the scalp. Note the superficial temporal a. (terminal branch of ECA) with frontal and parietal branches, occipital a., and posterior auricular a. from the external carotid.
🩸
Clinical Pearl: The scalp arteries supply little blood to the neurocranium itself — the cranial bones are supplied primarily by the middle meningeal artery. This is important because scalp lacerations bleed profusely but do not typically compromise blood supply to the skull.
04 / Venous Drainage

§ Veins of the Scalp

The scalp has two venous drainage systems that communicate with each other:

Dual Venous Drainage

SystemKey VeinsDrainage Path
Superficial (External)Superficial temporal v. → retromandibular v.; Frontal v. (supratrochlear); Occipital v.Retromandibular → common facial v. → internal jugular v. Occipital v. → external jugular v.
Deep (Intracranial)Emissary veins; Diploic veinsCommunicate through calvaria with dural venous sinuses (cavernous sinus, superior sagittal sinus, petrosal sinuses) → sigmoid sinus → internal jugular v.
VeinsVeins of the Scalp
Venous drainage showing superficial veins (supraorbital, supratrochlear, superficial temporal, occipital) and deep connections to dural sinuses (superior sagittal, transverse, sigmoid) via emissary and diploic veins.
⚠
Danger Zone — Emissary Veins: Emissary veins are valveless, allowing bidirectional flow. A superficial scalp infection can travel through emissary veins into the cavernous sinus or other dural sinuses, potentially causing life-threatening meningitis or cavernous sinus thrombosis.
05 / Lymphatic Drainage

§ Lymphatic Drainage

Lymph Node Groups

Node GroupRegion DrainedUltimate Drainage
Occipital nodesPosterior scalpAll → Deep cervical nodes
Retroauricular nodesPosterior temporal scalp, around auricle
Retromandibular nodesLateral scalp / parotid region
Anterior temporal nodesTemporal region
Submental nodesFrontal scalp (follows frontal vein inferiorly)→ Deep cervical nodes
LymphaticsLymphatic Drainage of the Scalp
Lymphatic drainage: occipital, retroauricular, infra-auricular, and retromandibular nodes all draining into the superior deep cervical nodes and jugular lymphatic trunk.
06 / Innervation

§ Nerves of the Scalp

Cutaneous (sensory) innervation of the scalp is divided by a coronal line through the ears: anterior to the auricles is trigeminal (CN V), posterior to the auricles is cervical spinal nerves (C2–C3).

Sensory Innervation

NerveDivisionTerritory
Supratrochlear n.V1 (Ophthalmic) V1Medial forehead; exits via supratrochlear foramen/notch
Supra-orbital n.V1 (Ophthalmic) V1Forehead and anterior scalp (medial & lateral branches); exits via supra-orbital foramen
Auriculotemporal n.V3 (Mandibular) V3Temporal region and most of lateral scalp
Small V2 twigsV2 (Maxillary)Small variable patch between V1 and V3 territories
Greater occipital n.Dorsal ramus C2 C2Posterior scalp (majority)
Lesser occipital n.Ventral ramus C2 C2Lateral posterior scalp, behind auricle
NervesNerves of the Scalp — Lateral View
Lateral view showing supraorbital, supratrochlear (V1), auriculotemporal (V3), greater & lesser occipital (C2) nerves.
TerritoriesCutaneous Nerve Territory Map
Color-coded oblique view: V1 frontal branches (red/orange region), auriculotemporal V3 (blue region), greater & lesser occipital C2 (blue, posterior), with small variable V2 territory (green).
⚡
Clinical Correlation: On a neurologic exam, V1 (supraorbital/supratrochlear) and V3 (auriculotemporal) are the main territories you test for the scalp. V2 contributes a small, variable patch that is difficult to detect sensory loss from. The posterior scalp is cervical (C2–C3), not trigeminal.
